topics.title: Introduction to Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT744 - PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — INTRODUCTION TO AUTOMATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Automation** is the deliberate application of a closed-loop, self-regulating combination of mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, electrical, electronic, and computer-based elements to an industrial process for the purpose of reducing the human intervention (both physical and cognitive) required to achieve and maintain prescribed process conditions, while delivering higher product quality, throughput, safety, repeatability, and economic efficiency.

In a more engineering-rigorous form, an automated system is one in which a set of **sensors**, **transmitters**, **controllers**, and **final control elements** form a closed information-and-energy loop such that the controlled variable tracks a **setpoint** without continuous operator supervision.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control on a Car

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and you set the cruise control to **90 km/h**.

| Human Action (Without Automation) | Automated Action (Cruise Control) |
|---|---|
| Eyes the speedometer continuously | **Sensor** continuously measures speed |
| Brain compares with target | **Controller** compares measured speed with setpoint |
| Foot adjusts accelerator pedal | **Final Control Element (throttle)** adjusts fuel |
| Reaction time ~ seconds | Reaction time ~ milliseconds |
| Tired, distracted | Never tires |

This is exactly how a **process control loop** operates in a chemical plant — measuring a process variable (temperature, pressure, flow, level) and manipulating another variable (steam flow, valve opening) to keep the process at the desired setpoint. The "driver" is replaced by a **PID controller** running inside a **Distributed Control System (DCS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction KTU Examiners Test Often**
> - **Mechanization** → replaces *human muscle* (e.g., conveyor, crane).
> - **Automation** → replaces *human thought + muscle* (sensor + decision + actuator loop).
> - **Computerization / Digitization** → uses *computers* to *support* human decision (still human-in-the-loop).
> - **Autonomy** → the system itself makes the decision loop closed with no human oversight.

### Standard Engineering Metrics for Automation

The KTU 2024 scheme lists the following quantifiable metrics for any automation study:

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — expressed in hours
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — expressed in hours
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** — expressed as %
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Payback Period** for cost justification
- **Loop Accuracy** — typically ±0.25 % of calibrated span
- **Signal Levels** — Industry standard: **4–20 mA DC** for analogue transmission (in engineering units)

---

## 2. History of Automation — Quick Timeline

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently set 3-mark questions on the "evolution of automation." Memorize the **three generations**.

| Generation | Era | Driving Technology | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Generation** | 1950s–1960s | Pneumatic & hydraulic controllers | Local single-loop controllers (Foxboro, Taylor) |
| **2nd Generation** | 1970s–1980s | Electronic analog + early DCS | **Honeywell TDC 2000** (1975) — first DCS |
| **3rd Generation** | 1990s–2000s | Digital microprocessors + PCs | PLC (Allen-Bradley PLC-5), open SCADA |
| **4th Generation (Industry 4.0)** | 2010–present | IoT, Cloud, AI, Edge computing | Cyber-Physical Systems, Digital Twin |
| **5th Generation (Industry 5.0)** | 2020+ | Human-Machine collaboration, Cobots | Sustainable, human-centric automation |

---

## 3. Why Automate? — The KTU 5-Pillar Justification

1. **Quality Consistency** — ±0.1 % repeatability vs ±1–2 % for human.
2. **Safety** — removes humans from hazardous zones (high P, high T, toxic).
3. **Economics** — lower variable cost; **OEE** rises from 60 % to 85 %.
4. **Throughput** — 24×7 operation; no shift fatigue.
5. **Information** — every variable is logged for analytics and regulatory compliance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 4. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Types of Automation

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes industrial automation into **three canonical types**. You must be able to draw and contrast these for any 7-mark question.

### 4.1 Fixed (Hard) Automation
A sequence of operations is **predetermined and hard-wired** into the equipment structure. Used for **very high volume, single-product** manufacturing.

> **Examples** in process industries:
> - Continuous distillation column control (steady-state operation).
> - Asphalt mixing plant.
> - Bottling line with dedicated rotary fillers.

**Characteristics:**
- Very high production rate (10⁴ – 10⁶ units/day).
- High initial capital investment.
- Inflexible to product change.
- Lowest per-unit variable cost.

### 4.2 Programmable Automation
The sequence of operations is **controlled by a program** (relay ladder, PLC ladder logic, structured text). Re-configurable but requires a **shutdown + reprogramming** cycle between batches.

> **Examples:**
> - Batch reactors in pharmaceutical plants.
> - CNC machining centre.
> - Robotic welding cell for a car body.

**Characteristics:**
- Suited for **low-to-medium volume, high-variety** production (10² – 10⁴ units).
- Investment lower than hard automation.
- Changeover time: minutes to hours.
- Core device: **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)**.

### 4.3 Flexible (Soft) Automation
An **extension of programmable automation** where changeovers are **automatic** (no production stoppage). Human-coded instructions are downloaded, and the system re-configures itself.

> **Examples:**
> - Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) with AGVs and robotic cells.
> - Computer-integrated batch recipe execution in a DCS.
> - Modern bottling line that switches SKU via a touch-screen.

**Characteristics:**
- Medium volume, medium variety.
- Investment highest, but utilization is also high.
- The "ideal" for modern **Industry 4.0** plants.

### 4.4 Comparative Matrix (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS)

| Parameter | Fixed | Programmable | Flexible |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | Very high | Low–medium | Medium |
| Variety | Very low | Medium | High |
| Investment | High | Medium | Very high |
| Re-programmability | None | Yes (manual) | Yes (automatic) |
| Typical device | Cam, sequencer | PLC, DCS | FMS, CIM |
| Example | Oil refinery column | Batch reactor | FMS line |

### 4.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even though Module 1 is descriptive, KTU examiners often drop a **numerical part (a)** worth 4 marks. Memorize these equations.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = P_{SP} - P_{PV}$ | Error signal | engineering units |
| 2 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Control error in standard form | engineering units |
| 3 | $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$ | Transfer function | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_v(s)$ | Open-loop TF product | dimensionless |
| 5 | $G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)\,H(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Availability × Performance × Quality) | % |
| 7 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n}\dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value of automation project | ₹ |
| 8 | $I_{4\text{-}20} = I_{min} + \left(\dfrac{V - V_{min}}{V_{max}-V_{min}}\right)\times 16$ | 4–20 mA signal scaling to PV | mA |
| 9 | $H = \dfrac{\%\,\text{increase in output}}{\%\,\text{increase in input}}$ | Sensitivity / Process Gain | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Span} = \text{URV} - \text{LRV}$ | Instrument range | engineering units |

> **Notation guide:** $P_{SP}$ = setpoint, $P_{PV}$ = process variable, $r(t)$ = reference, $y(t)$ = output, $G_c$ = controller TF, $G_p$ = process TF, $G_v$ = valve TF, $H$ = feedback element, $I_{min}=4\,\text{mA}$, $I_{max}=20\,\text{mA}$, $V$ = measured variable, URV/LRV = Upper/Lower Range Value.

### 4.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

The above classification drives **plant layout decisions** in KTU's own process industry clusters in Kerala (Cochin refinery, FACT, HOCL). For a **bulk chemical** like ammonia, *fixed* automation is chosen. For a **pharma** plant in Kakkanchery, *flexible* automation is mandatory because the product mix changes daily.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 5. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 5.1 Worked Example 1 — 4–20 mA Signal Scaling (Direct KTU numerical)

**Problem (3 marks):** A temperature transmitter has a calibrated range of **200 °C to 600 °C** and outputs a **4–20 mA** signal. The controller reads **12 mA**. What is the measured temperature?

**Step 1 — Write the standard linearization equation.**

$$I = I_{min} + \left(\dfrac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max}-T_{min}}\right)\times (I_{max}-I_{min})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$12 = 4 + \left(\dfrac{T - 200}{600-200}\right)\times (20-4)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constants.**

$$12 - 4 = \left(\dfrac{T - 200}{400}\right)\times 16$$

$$8 = \dfrac{16(T-200)}{400}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate T.**

$$8 \times 400 = 16(T-200)$$

$$3200 = 16(T-200)$$

$$T-200 = 200$$

$$\boxed{T = 400\,^\circ\text{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** 12 mA is the **exact midpoint** of 4–20 mA (midpoint = 12 mA). Therefore, T must be the midpoint of 200–600 °C, which is **400 °C**. Confirmed ✓.

### 5.2 Worked Example 2 — Block Diagram Reduction (4-mark KTU favourite)

**Problem:** A process has the following blocks: $G_c(s) = 2$ (controller), $G_p(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+1}$ (process), $G_v(s) = 0.5$ (valve). Feedback transducer is unity $H(s) = 1$. Find the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$.

**Step 1 — Write the open-loop transfer function.**

$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s)\cdot G_p(s)\cdot G_v(s) = 2 \times \dfrac{5}{s+1} \times 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.**

$$G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard unity-feedback closed-loop formula.**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G_{OL}(s)}{1+G_{OL}(s)\,H(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{5}{s+1}}{1 + \dfrac{5}{s+1}\cdot 1}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(s+1)$.**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+1+5} = \dfrac{5}{s+6}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{5}{s+6}}$$

### 5.3 Worked Example 3 — OEE Calculation (Industry 4.0 favourite)

**Problem:** A packaging line operates for **480 minutes/day** but produces only **30,000 good units** out of a theoretical **40,000 units** at design speed. Availability = **85 %**, Performance = **90 %**. Find the **OEE** and rank it on the **world-class benchmark** scale.

**Step 1 — Convert availability to decimal.**

$$A = 85\% = 0.85$$

**Step 2 — Convert performance to decimal.**

$$P = 90\% = 0.90$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality from the good-unit data.**

$$Q = \dfrac{30{,}000}{40{,}000} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE.**

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.75$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.57375 = 57.4\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the world-class benchmark.**

| OEE | Class |
|---|---|
| < 40 % | Inexcusable |
| 40–60 % | Typical |
| 60–85 % | Best-in-class |
| > 85 % | **World-class** (Toyota, Intel) |

Our plant at **57.4 %** is **typical** — there is **substantial room** to invest in automation (predictive maintenance, vision-based quality inspection).

### 5.4 Sensitivity / Process Gain Derivation

A sensor input $x$ produces output $y$ according to $y = Kx + b$ where $K$ is the static gain.

**Step 1 — Define gain.**

$$K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \text{slope of the calibration curve}$$

**Step 2 — For a differential element,**

$$K = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x_0}$$

**Step 3 — For a transmitter, $K$ is fixed by the span and the current range:**

$$K_{TX} = \dfrac{I_{max}-I_{min}}{T_{max}-T_{min}} = \dfrac{16\,\text{mA}}{400\,^\circ\text{C}} = 0.04\,\text{mA/}^\circ\text{C}$$

This is the **sensitivity** the controller will use when converting current back to engineering units.

### 5.5 Cost Justification of an Automation Project (NPV method)

A chemical plant invests **₹50,00,000** in a DCS upgrade. Annual cash inflow from labour + quality savings = **₹15,00,000** for 5 years. Discount rate **r = 10 %**. Should the company invest?

**Step 1 — Write NPV formula.**

$$\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{5}\dfrac{\text{CF}}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each year.**

| Year $t$ | CF | Discount factor $(1.1)^t$ | PV |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15,00,000 | 1.10 | 13,63,636 |
| 2 | 15,00,000 | 1.21 | 12,39,669 |
| 3 | 15,00,000 | 1.331 | 11,26,972 |
| 4 | 15,00,000 | 1.4641 | 10,24,520 |
| 5 | 15,00,000 | 1.61051 | 9,31,382 |

**Step 3 — Sum the present values.**

$$\sum \text{PV} = 13{,}63{,}636 + 12{,}39{,}669 + 11{,}26{,}972 + 10{,}24{,}520 + 9{,}31{,}382$$

$$\sum \text{PV} = 56{,}86{,}179$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV.**

$$\text{NPV} = -50{,}00{,}000 + 56{,}86{,}179 = \boxed{+6{,}86{,}179\,\text{(Positive)}}$$

**Conclusion:** Since **NPV > 0**, the automation project is **financially justified**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners will accept an **annualized shortcut** using the annuity factor $A_{n,r} = \dfrac{(1+r)^n-1}{r(1+r)^n}$. For $n=5$, $r=10\%$, $A = 3.7908$. Then $\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \text{CF}\times A$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 6. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 6.1 Generic Block Diagram of a Process Automation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint SP] --> C[Controller Gc s]
    D[Disturbance D s] --> EP[Process Gp s]
    C --> FC[Final Control Element Valve Gv s]
    FC --> EP
    EP --> P[Process Variable PV]
    P --> TX[Transmitter H s]
    TX --> CMP[Comparator Summing Junction]
    A --> CMP
    CMP --> C
    P -.feedback.-> CMP

    classDef src fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef dec fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef act fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef meas fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef p fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px

    class A,CMP,C src
    class FC act
    class TX meas
    class EP,P p
    class D dec
```

```mermaid

    P --> P1[Batch reactor pharmaceutical]

```

### 6.3 Automation Pyramid — ISA-95 Functional Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[L5 ERP - Business Planning] --> L4
    L4[L4 MES - Manufacturing Execution] --> L3
    L3[L3 SCADA / HMI - Supervisory Control] --> L2
    L2[L2 PLC / DCS - Control] --> L1
    L1[L1 Sensors Actuators Instrumentation] --> L0
    L0[L0 Physical Process Plant]

    style L5 fill:#311b92,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L4 fill:#1a237e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#e65100,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L0 fill:#3e2723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The ISA-95 (or Purdue) pyramid is **high-yield**. A 7-mark question on "levels of automation in a process plant" almost always expects this diagram with one example of equipment at each level.

### 6.4 Industry 4.0 Components Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CP[Cyber Physical Systems] --> IOT[IoT Sensors]
    CP --> CC[Cloud Computing]
    CP --> AI[AI and Machine Learning]
    CP --> DT[Digital Twin]
    CP --> BC[Big Data Analytics]
    CP --> AR[Augmented Reality]
    CP --> AM[Additive Manufacturing]
    CP --> CR[Cobots Collaborative Robots]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 7. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1a]**

**Q1. (a)** Define automation. Distinguish between **mechanization** and **automation** with one example each. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Automation** is the use of a self-regulating closed-loop system of sensors, controllers, and actuators to perform a process with **minimal human intervention**, thereby improving quality, safety, and productivity.
>
> - **Mechanization:** Replaces *human physical effort* by machines. The decision-making is still by the human. *Example:* a power-driven conveyor belt in a bottling plant moves bottles automatically, but the operator still starts/stops it and decides speed.
> - **Automation:** Replaces *both* human effort and *human decision-making* by a feedback control loop. *Example:* the cruise control of a car automatically maintains speed without driver intervention.
>
> **Key line to remember:** "Mechanization gives muscles to humans; automation gives brains to machines."

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1b]**

**Q1. (b)** List the **three main types of automation** and state **two applications** of each in a process plant. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Application 1 (Process Industry) | Application 2 (Discrete Industry) |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed | Continuous distillation column | Dedicated rotary bottle filler |
| Programmable | Batch reactor (ISA-88 recipe) | CNC machining centre |
| Flexible | SKU-switchable bottling line | FMS with AGVs and robots |

---

### PART B — Full-Question Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice, Q7A]**

**Q7. A.** (a) Draw the **ISA-95 automation pyramid** for a process plant and explain the function of **each level** with **one example of hardware/software** at each level. **(7 marks)**

(b) A temperature transmitter has a range of **100 °C to 300 °C** and outputs **4–20 mA**. The DCS receives **14.4 mA**. Calculate the **measured temperature**. If the controller setpoint is **200 °C**, what is the **error signal** and its **sign convention**? **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**(a) ISA-95 Pyramid — Draw the figure first (refer Section 6.3 above), then describe each level:**

- **Level 0 — Physical Process:** The actual plant equipment (vessels, pumps, columns, heat exchangers, pipes).
- **Level 1 — Instrumentation / Sensors & Actuators:** RTDs, thermocouples, orifice meters, control valves. *Hardware example:* PT-100 RTD.
- **Level 2 — Control (PLC / DCS):** Real-time loop control, interlocks, sequential logic. *Software example:* Honeywell Experion PKS.
- **Level 3 — Supervisory (SCADA / HMI):** Operator screens, alarms, trends. *Software example:* WinCC, iFIX.
- **Level 4 — Manufacturing Execution System (MES):** Batch tracking, recipe management, OEE. *Software example:* Siemens SIMATIC IT.
- **Level 5 — Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP):** Order, finance, supply chain. *Software example:* SAP S/4HANA.

**Information flow is vertical;** commands flow **downward** (ERP → L0) and data flows **upward** (L0 → ERP).

**(b) Temperature calculation**

**Step 1 — Write the linearization equation.**

$$I = I_{min} + \left(\dfrac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max}-T_{min}}\right)\times 16$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$14.4 = 4 + \left(\dfrac{T - 100}{200}\right)\times 16$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$10.4 = \dfrac{16(T-100)}{200}$$

$$10.4 \times 200 = 16(T-100)$$

$$2080 = 16(T-100)$$

$$T - 100 = 130$$

$$\boxed{T = 230\,^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Step 4 — Error signal.**

$$e = \text{SP} - \text{PV} = 200 - 230 = -30\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 5 — Sign interpretation.**

A **negative** error means the **PV is *above*** the setpoint. The controller must take a **reverse** action — close the heating steam valve. In standard KTU convention, $e = r - y$ (setpoint minus measured) is used.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice, Q7B]**

**Q7. B.** (a) Explain the **three types of industrial automation** with a **comparative table** and a **real-world example** of each. Justify which type is most suitable for a **modern pharmaceutical batch plant** manufacturing 50 SKUs. **(7 marks)**

(b) With a **neat block diagram**, derive the **closed-loop transfer function** $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ for a unity-feedback system with $G_c(s)=4$, $G_p(s)=\dfrac{2}{s+3}$, $G_v(s)=0.5$. What is the **steady-state error** for a unit-step input? **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**(a) Three types — see Section 4.1 to 4.4 above for the table and descriptions.**

**Justification for the pharmaceutical batch plant (50 SKUs):**

The product has **high variety** (50 different formulations) and **low-to-medium volume** for each. A *fixed* automation cannot changeover. A *programmable* PLC/DCS can be reconfigured per batch, but changeover is manual. Therefore, **flexible automation** is the correct choice — the DCS supports **ISA-88 recipes** and the plant can switch from one SKU to the next by uploading a new recipe, with **automated CIP (clean-in-place)** and **line clearance**. Throughput and variety are simultaneously optimized.

**(b) Block diagram — see Section 6.1 above.**

**Step 1 — Forward path gain.**

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) = 4 \times \dfrac{2}{s+3} \times 0.5 = \dfrac{4}{s+3}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function.**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{4}{s+3}}{1+\dfrac{4}{s+3}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(s+3)$.**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{4}{s+3+4} = \dfrac{4}{s+7}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state error for a unit-step input (Final Value Theorem).**

The system is **Type 0** (no integrator in the loop). The position error constant is:

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\dfrac{4}{s+3} = \dfrac{4}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Compute steady-state error.**

$$e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p} = \dfrac{1}{1+\dfrac{4}{3}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{7}{3}} = \dfrac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

$$\boxed{e_{ss} \approx 0.4286 \text{ (or } 42.86\,\%\text{)}}$$

**Interpretation:** A unit-step setpoint is followed with a permanent offset of ~0.43 units. To eliminate this, an **integral (I) action** must be added — which is why **PID controllers** are universal in process automation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks**
> 1. **Don't write** "Automation = Mechanization" — it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. Use the precise **sensors + controller + actuator** triad definition.
> 2. **Sign of error:** KTU strictly follows $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. Writing the wrong sign will cost 2 marks in derivations.
> 3. **In 4–20 mA problems,** always state that **4 mA = 0 % (live zero)**, not 0 mA. This **live-zero** is a safety feature (4 mA indicates a live wire, 0 mA indicates a wire break).
> 4. **In block diagrams,** always label **disturbance D(s)** entering at the process — forgetting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **OEE = A × P × Q**, not A + P + Q. Many students add them; the correct operator is **multiplication**.
> 6. For **closed-loop TF**, multiply the forward path by $G_{OL} / (1 + G_{OL}\,H)$ — not the other way around.
> 7. For **Type-0 systems**, never say the steady-state error is zero. It always exists for a step input.
> 8. In the **ISA-95 pyramid**, Level 0 is the *process*, not the sensors. Sensors are Level 1.

---

## 8. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam).**

- **Definition:** Automation = sensor + controller + actuator + feedback in a **closed loop** to minimize human intervention.
- **Three Types:** Fixed (high volume, low variety) — Programmable (PLC, low–medium) — Flexible (FMS, high variety, automatic changeover).
- **5 Pillars of Justification:** Quality, Safety, Economics, Throughput, Information.
- **Generations:** Pneumatic → Electronic Analog → Digital DCS → IoT/Industry 4.0 → Industry 5.0 (human-centric cobots).
- **Standard Signal:** **4–20 mA** with **live zero** at 4 mA. Span = 16 mA corresponds to the engineering range.
- **Block-Diagram elements:** Setpoint → Comparator → Controller $G_c$ → Final Control Element $G_v$ → Process $G_p$ → PV → Transmitter $H$ → back to comparator. Disturbance $D$ enters the process.
- **Closed-Loop TF:** $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_{OL}(s)}{1+G_{OL}(s)H(s)}$.
- **OEE** = A × P × Q. World-class is **> 85 %**.
- **NPV > 0** ⇒ project is financially justified; use $r$ = discount rate.
- **Sensitivity K** = $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$; for 4–20 mA, $K = \dfrac{16\,\text{mA}}{\text{span (eng. units)}}$.
- **ISA-95 Levels:** L0 Process, L1 Sensors/Actuators, L2 PLC/DCS, L3 SCADA/HMI, L4 MES, L5 ERP.
- **Industry 4.0 components:** CPS, IoT, Cloud, AI/ML, Digital Twin, Big Data, AR/VR, Additive Mfg, Cobots.
- **Live-zero safety:** wire break = 0 mA = alarm; live 4 mA = healthy.
- **Setpoint = target**, **PV = measured**, **error = SP − PV** (KTU convention).
- **Type-0 system → step error is non-zero**; add integral action to remove it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of Industrial Automation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT744 - PROCESS AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Architecture of Industrial Automation Systems ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Industrial Automation Architecture** is the formally structured, layered arrangement of hardware, software, communication networks, and control algorithms that work in concert to monitor, regulate, and optimize industrial processes with minimal or zero human intervention. As per the **ISA-95 / IEC 62264 standard** (also referred to as the **Purdue Reference Model**), this architecture is partitioned into well-defined hierarchical levels — each level performing a distinct class of functions ranging from physical sensing/actuation to enterprise-level business decisions. Mathematically, an automation architecture can be represented as a five-tuple: $$\mathcal{A} = \{L, C, S, N, P\}$$ where: - $L$ = Set of hierarchical levels $\{L_0, L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4\}$ - $C$ = Set of control components (sensors, controllers, actuators) - $S$ = Set of software services (SCADA, HMI, MES, ERP) - $N$ = Set of communication networks (Fieldbus, Ethernet, Wireless) - $P$ = Set of protocols governing data exchange (Modbus, Profibus, OPC-UA, EtherNet/IP) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The architecture is NOT just hardware — it is an *integrated framework* of **Level 0 (Process) → Level 1 (Basic Control) → Level 2 (Supervisory) → Level 3 (Operations Management) → Level 4 (Enterprise)**. Examiners frequently test the **function and components of each level** and the **flow of information** between levels. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body" Imagine an industrial plant as a **human body**: | Human Body | Industrial Plant Equivalent | |---|---| | **Nerve endings** (touch, pain) | **Sensors / Transmitters** (Level 0) — feel temperature, pressure, flow | | **Spinal cord / reflexes** | **PLCs / DCS Controllers** (Level 1) — instant local decisions | | **Brain stem & conscious mind** | **SCADA / HMI** (Level 2) — operator awareness & supervisory commands | | **Hippocampus (memory & planning)** | **MES — Manufacturing Execution Systems** (Level 3) — batch records, scheduling | | **Prefrontal cortex (strategic thinking)** | **ERP — Enterprise Resource Planning** (Level 4) — business decisions, finances | Just as signals travel from your finger (sensor) up through the spinal cord to the brain (controller) and back down with a decision (actuator), data in industrial automation flows **upward** (telemetry) and **commands flow downward** (setpoints). > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** Higher levels have **slower response times** but **broader scope**; lower levels have **faster response times** but **narrower scope**. This is the **fundamental trade-off** in any automation hierarchy. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the Topic - **Sampling Rate:** typically **10 Hz to 1000 Hz** at the field level - **Control Loop Execution Time:** **1 ms to 100 ms** at Level 1 - **SCADA Refresh Time:** **1 s to 10 s** at Level 2 - **ERP Transaction Time:** **minutes to hours** at Level 4 - **Standard Reference Model:** **ISA-95 / IEC 62264 / Purdue Reference Model** > [!NOTE] > The ISA-95 standard is the **only internationally accepted model** that KTU examiners expect you to draw and label. Memorize all five levels and their typical components. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Level Hierarchy (Purdue Reference Model / ISA-95) The architecture is best understood as a vertical stack of **five functional levels**, separated by **four logical boundaries** (as illustrated in Section 4). Each level has a clearly demarcated responsibility. --- #### **Level 0 — The Process / Physical Layer** - **What it is:** The actual physical plant — pipes, vessels, motors, valves, reactors. - **Components:** - **Sensors / Transmitters:** RTDs, thermocouples, orifice plates, Coriolis flow meters, pressure transmitters, level switches. - **Actuators / Final Control Elements:** Pneumatic control valves, motorized VFDs, solenoid valves, dampers, variable-speed pumps. - **Signal Types:** **4–20 mA current loops**, **0–10 V**, **HART**, **fieldbus digital signals**, **pneumatic 3–15 psi**. - **Function:** Convert physical phenomena → standardized electrical/pneumatic signals, and convert control signals → physical action. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trap:** A *transmitter* is **NOT** the same as a *sensor*. A *sensor* senses; a *transmitter* converts the sensor's raw output (mV, Ω) into a standardized **4–20 mA** signal. Examiners love this distinction. --- #### **Level 1 — Basic Control Layer** - **What it is:** The "reflex arc" of the plant. Devices that execute closed-loop control in real time. - **Components:** - **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** — discrete/repetitive logic, ladder logic, function blocks. - **DCS (Distributed Control System)** — continuous process control (e.g., Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV, ABB 800xA). - **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** — geographically distributed, telemetry-oriented. - **Single-loop / Multi-loop Controllers** — dedicated PID hardware. - **Control Algorithms:** **PID**, cascade, ratio, feedforward, override (select) control, model-predictive. - **Scan Time / Cycle Time:** Typically **10 ms – 1 s**. --- #### **Level 2 — Supervisory Control Layer** - **What it is:** The "operator's window" into the plant. - **Components:** - **SCADA** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) — for geographically distributed assets. - **HMI** (Human–Machine Interface) — graphical mimics, trends, alarms. - **Historians** — long-term time-series data storage (e.g., OSIsoft PI). - **Alarm Management Systems.** - **Functions:** Operator setpoint changes, alarm acknowledgement, batch visualization, trending. --- #### **Level 3 — Operations / Manufacturing Execution Layer** - **Components:** **MES** (Manufacturing Execution System) — production tracking, batch genealogy, OEE, recipe management. - **Functions:** Decouples plant-floor reality from business planning. Translates "make 10,000 kg of grade-A polymer today" into "start reactor R-201 at 06:00 with recipe R-A1." --- #### **Level 4 — Enterprise / Business Planning Layer** - **Components:** **ERP** (Enterprise Resource Planning) — SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics. - **Functions:** Long-horizon planning — supply chain, procurement, finance, HR, sales forecasting, regulatory reporting. --- ### 2.2 Alternative Architectural Topologies Beyond the hierarchy, automation architectures can be classified by **topology**: 1. **Centralized Architecture** — One large computer controls everything. Obsolete except in very small systems. Vulnerable to single-point failure. 2. **Decentralized (Distributed) Architecture** — Each subsystem has its own controller. Robust, scalable, and modular. **Industry standard today.** 3. **Hierarchical (Networked) Architecture** — Combines layers 0–4 with networks between them. **Most common in modern plants.** 4. **Hybrid Architecture** — Mixes centralized and distributed elements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The current industry standard is a **Hybrid / Hierarchical Distributed** architecture. Be prepared to draw this and justify why distributed beats centralized for fault tolerance. --- ### 2.3 Communication Protocols — The "Languages" of Each Level | Level | Typical Protocols | Media | |---|---|---| | L0–L1 (Field) | **4–20 mA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus PA, IO-Link** | Twisted pair, dedicated cable | | L1–L2 (Control) | **Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC-UA** | Industrial Ethernet | | L2–L3 (Supervisory–MES) | **OPC-UA, SQL/ODBC, MQTT** | Ethernet / TCP-IP | | L3–L4 (MES–ERP) | **REST APIs, SOAP, EDI, B2MML (ISA-95 XML)** | Corporate WAN | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Current loop signal** | $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{V - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}}$ | mA | Live-zero principle | | **Control loop latency** | $T_{loop} = T_{sensor} + T_{controller} + T_{actuator}$ | s | Sum of subsystem delays | | **PID output** | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt} + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt$ | % | ISA standard form | | **Closed-loop bandwidth** | $f_{BW} \approx \dfrac{0.35}{t_{settle}}$ | Hz | Empirical rule | | **Alarm rate (per operator)** | $A_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{alarms}}{T_{shift}}$ | alarms/hr | Should be $<$ **6/hr** (EEMUA 191) | | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Sensor quality metric | | **Information flow rate** | $R = n \cdot f_s \cdot \log_2(M)$ | bits/s | $n$ tags, $f_s$ sample rate, $M$ levels | | **Plant availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | Target $>$ **99.9%** | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU does **not** require you to memorize every formula, but you must know the **4–20 mA live-zero equation**, the **PID output equation**, and the **closed-loop bandwidth vs. settling time** relation. These appear in nearly every ESE. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This architecture is the **backbone** of: - **Oil & Gas:** Refineries, offshore platforms (DCS-dominated). - **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch automation under FDA 21 CFR Part 11 (MES-dominated). - **Power Generation:** Boiler control, turbine-governor systems. - **Discrete Manufacturing:** Automotive body-in-white (PLC-dominated). - **Water Treatment:** SCADA over long geographical spans. - **Smart Manufacturing / Industry 4.0:** Adds **IIoT**, **cloud**, and **digital twins** as a parallel layer over the classical hierarchy. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Information Flow Rate Through the Hierarchy **Problem Setup:** A Level-1 PLC is monitoring 64 analog tags from Level-0 transmitters. Each tag has a 12-bit ADC resolution, sampled at 10 Hz. Calculate the bit-rate from Level-0 → Level-1, and the total bandwidth required at the L1→L2 link if the SCADA polls 4 such PLCs every 1 s. --- **Step 1 — Define the per-tag bit-rate.** Each tag carries $b = 12$ bits per sample, sampled at $f_s = 10$ Hz. $$R_{tag} = b \cdot f_s = 12 \times 10 = 120 \text{ bits/s}$$ **Explanation:** Every 100 ms, the ADC emits a 12-bit word representing the measured value. This is the *raw* information rate from one sensor. --- **Step 2 — Compute the total L0 → L1 bit-rate for 64 tags.** $$R_{L0 \to L1} = n \cdot R_{tag} = 64 \times 120$$ $$R_{L0 \to L1} = 7{,}680 \text{ bits/s} = 7.68 \text{ kbps}$$ **Explanation:** The 64 tags are time-multiplexed onto the fieldbus. Modern protocols (e.g., EtherNet/IP) can carry this trivially. --- **Step 3 — Convert to bytes per second for practical insight.** $$R_{bytes} = \dfrac{R_{L0 \to L1}}{8} = \dfrac{7{,}680}{8} = 960 \text{ B/s}$$ **Explanation:** This is roughly **1 kB per second** — extremely low by modern standards. A 1990-era 10 Mbps Ethernet could carry **10,000x** this load. --- **Step 4 — Compute the L1 → L2 link load (SCADA polling 4 PLCs).** If SCADA polls each PLC at 1 Hz and reads all 64 tags per poll: $$R_{L1 \to L2} = 4 \text{ PLCs} \times 1 \text{ poll/s} \times 64 \text{ tags} \times 12 \text{ bits}$$ $$R_{L1 \to L2} = 3{,}072 \text{ bits/s} = 3.072 \text{ kbps}$$ **Explanation:** Notice that L1 → L2 is *less* than L0 → L1 because SCADA polls are typically sub-sampled (it doesn't need every single intermediate value). This is a real example of the **information reduction** that occurs as data moves up the hierarchy. --- **Step 5 — Compute the total bandwidth utilization on a 100 Mbps Ethernet link.** $$\eta = \dfrac{R_{L1 \to L2}}{R_{link}} = \dfrac{3{,}072}{100 \times 10^6} \times 100\%$$ $$\eta = 3.072 \times 10^{-3}\,\%$$ **Explanation:** Industrial Ethernet is *enormously* over-provisioned for process data. This is why deterministic protocols (PROFINET, EtherCAT) are used — they trade raw bandwidth for **bounded latency**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Closed-Loop Bandwidth from Settling Time **Engineering rule of thumb:** For an underdamped second-order system with 5% settling criterion: $$t_{settle} \approx \dfrac{3}{\zeta \omega_n}$$ The closed-loop bandwidth (–3 dB frequency) is empirically: $$f_{BW} \approx \dfrac{0.35}{t_{settle}}$$ **Example:** A temperature control loop settles in 5 minutes = 300 s. $$f_{BW} = \dfrac{0.35}{300} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Hz} \approx 1.2 \text{ mHz}$$ **Interpretation:** The controller only needs to update at roughly **2–3× this**, i.e., **3–5 mHz**, or once every 3–5 minutes. This justifies the **slow scan rate** at Level 2 and the **fast scan rate** at Level 1. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Architectural Trade-offs | Architecture | Fault Tolerance | Scalability | Latency | Cost | Complexity | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Centralized** | **Low** (single point of failure) | Poor | Low (in theory) | High initial | High | | **Decentralized (DCS)** | **High** (redundant controllers) | Excellent | Bounded | High | Moderate | | **Hierarchical (PLC + SCADA)** | **Medium–High** | Excellent | Variable per level | Moderate | Moderate | | **Hybrid (DCS + PLC islands + IIoT)** | **Very High** | Excellent | Layer-dependent | High | High | | **Fully Centralized Cloud (Industry 4.0 extreme)** | **Low** (network-dependent) | Excellent | High (network-bound) | Low (CAPEX) | High (cyber risk) | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model — Hierarchical Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef enterprise fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef operations fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef supervisory fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef control fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef network fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 L4["LEVEL 4 - Enterprise
ERP - SAP - Oracle
Functions: Finance, Procurement, Sales, HR"]:::enterprise L3["LEVEL 3 - Operations Management
MES - OEE - Batch Records
Functions: Production Scheduling, Genealogy"]:::operations L2["LEVEL 2 - Supervisory Control
SCADA - HMI - Historian
Functions: Operator Interface, Trends, Alarms"]:::supervisory L1["LEVEL 1 - Basic Control
PLC - DCS Controller - RTU
Functions: PID, Logic, Cascade, Sequencing"]:::control L0["LEVEL 0 - Physical Process
Sensors, Transmitters, Actuators, Valves, Motors
Functions: Sense, Measure, Manipulate"]:::process N3["Corporate WAN
REST API - B2MML - EDI"]:::network N2["Plant Ethernet
OPC-UA - SQL - MQTT"]:::network N1["Control Network
PROFINET - EtherNet-IP - Modbus TCP"]:::network N0["Field Network
4-20mA - HART - Foundation Fieldbus - IO-Link"]:::network L4 --- N3 L3 --- N2 L2 --- N1 L1 --- N0 L0 --- N0 L4 <-->|Business Plan / Reports| L3 L3 <-->|Production Orders / Results| L2 L2 <-->|Setpoints / Telemetry| L1 L1 <-->|Control Signals / Measurements| L0 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Bottom-up arrows** carry **measurements and reports** (data flowing toward the enterprise). - **Top-down arrows** carry **setpoints, schedules, and recipes** (commands flowing toward the process). - The **yellow blocks** are the **communication networks** that join the levels — they are the *glue* of the architecture. --- ### 4.2 Functional Flow — From Sensor to ERP ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef sensor fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef control fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef hmi fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef biz fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#000,color:#000 S1[Sensor: RTD]:::sensor S2[Transmitter: 4-20 mA]:::sensor S3[PLC: PID Algorithm]:::control S4[DCS Controller]:::control S5[SCADA HMI Screen]:::hmi S6[Historian Database]:::hmi S7[MES Batch Record]:::biz S8[ERP Order Status]:::biz S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 S8 -. Setpoint .-> S5 S5 -. Recipe .-> S3 ``` **Explanation:** A temperature reading from an RTD is amplified and linearized by the transmitter, executed by the PLC's PID, supervised by the DCS, displayed on the HMI, archived in the historian, contextualized in the MES batch record, and finally aggregated into the ERP's order status. --- ### 4.3 Comparison: Centralized vs Distributed Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CENTRAL["Centralized Architecture - OBSOLETE"] direction TB CC[Mainframe Computer] T1[Transmitter 1] --> CC T2[Transmitter 2] --> CC T3[Transmitter 3] --> CC CC --> A1[Actuator 1] CC --> A2[Actuator 2] CC --> A3[Actuator 3] end subgraph DISTRIB["Distributed Architecture - MODERN STANDARD"] direction TB PC1[Controller 1] PC2[Controller 2] PC3[Controller 3] T1B[Transmitter 1] --> PC1 --> A1B[Actuator 1] T2B[Transmitter 2] --> PC2 --> A2B[Actuator 2] T3B[Transmitter 3] --> PC3 --> A3B[Actuator 3] PC1 <--> PC2 <--> PC3 end ``` **Reading:** In the centralized model, *one* failure halts the entire plant. In the distributed model, a single controller failure affects only its own loop — the rest continues operating. This is the **fault-tolerance argument** you will write in every answer. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define Industrial Automation Architecture. List the five levels of the ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model with one example component at each level.* **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Industrial Automation Architecture is a structured, layered framework of hardware, software, and communication networks that collectively monitor, control, and optimize industrial processes. It is standardized by **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** as the Purdue Reference Model. **Five Levels (2 marks):** | Level | Name | Example Component | |---|---|---| | L0 | Physical Process | RTD temperature sensor | | L1 | Basic Control | PLC / DCS Controller | | L2 | Supervisory Control | SCADA / HMI | | L3 | Operations Management | MES (Manufacturing Execution System) | | L4 | Enterprise | ERP (e.g., SAP) | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 2]** *Differentiate between PLC, DCS, and SCADA. State one industrial application where each is most suited.* **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** | System | Full Form | Strength | Best Suited For | |---|---|---|---| | **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller | High-speed discrete logic | Automotive assembly line | | **DCS** | Distributed Control System | Tightly integrated continuous control | Oil refinery (continuous process) | | **SCADA** | Supervisory Control & Data Acquisition | Wide-area telemetry | National power grid / water distribution | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]* **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an Industrial Automation System** based on the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model**. Describe the function of each level and the nature of information flow between levels. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **Centralized, Decentralized, and Hierarchical** automation architectures. Justify why a **distributed (decentralized) architecture is preferred in modern process plants**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model (7 marks):** The ISA-95 model partitions the automation system into **five functional levels**, each with a distinct responsibility. The information flow is **bidirectional** — measurements flow upward, setpoints flow downward. - **Level 0 – Physical Process (1 mark):** Contains the actual plant equipment — vessels, pipes, pumps, motors. **Sensors** (RTDs, pressure transmitters, flow meters) measure physical variables; **actuators** (control valves, VFDs, solenoid valves) manipulate them. Signals are typically **4–20 mA** or digital fieldbus. - **Level 1 – Basic Control (1 mark):** Contains **PLCs, DCS controllers, and RTUs**. These execute real-time control algorithms — **PID, cascade, ratio, feedforward, override** — with cycle times of 10 ms to 1 s. They directly close the feedback loop with Level 0. - **Level 2 – Supervisory Control (1 mark):** Contains the **SCADA, HMI, and Historian**. The HMI provides the graphical interface for the operator. The Historian stores time-series data. Operators issue setpoint changes from this level. - **Level 3 – Operations Management (1 mark):** Contains the **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)**. It manages production scheduling, batch genealogy, OEE tracking, and quality records. It decouples the plant floor from business planning. - **Level 4 – Enterprise (1 mark):** Contains the **ERP system** (SAP, Oracle). It handles business functions — procurement, finance, sales, HR, and long-horizon planning. - **Information Flow (2 marks):** The data flow is **bidirectional**. From L0→L1→L2→L3→L4, the information becomes **more aggregated and slower** in update rate (Hz → kHz → seconds → minutes). From L4→L3→L2→L1→L0, the commands become **more specific and faster** in execution (business plans → production orders → setpoints → actuator signals). The **networks** between levels use different protocols — 4–20 mA / HART at L0–L1, industrial Ethernet at L1–L2, and corporate WAN at L3–L4. *(Block diagram to be drawn — see Section 4.1.)* --- **(b) Comparison & Justification of Distributed Architecture (7 marks):** | Architecture | Description | Fault Tolerance | Scalability | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Centralized** | One computer controls entire plant | **Poor** — single point of failure | Poor | Legacy / very small systems | | **Decentralized (Distributed)** | Each loop has its own controller; controllers networked | **Excellent** — failure is local | Excellent | Modern DCS-based plants | | **Hierarchical** | Layers 0–4 organized vertically with networks between | **High** | Excellent | Industry standard | **Justification (4 marks):** The **distributed architecture is preferred** in modern process plants for the following reasons: 1. **Fault Tolerance:** A failure in one controller affects only that loop; the rest of the plant continues operating. This avoids catastrophic single-point-of-failure shutdowns. 2. **Scalability:** New units, reactors, or skids can be added simply by installing a new controller and connecting it to the network — without disturbing the existing system. 3. **Reduced Cabling:** Fieldbus and industrial Ethernet replace point-to-point 4–20 mA wiring, drastically reducing installation cost and maintenance burden. 4. **Geographic Flexibility:** Suitable for large, geographically dispersed plants (refineries, pipelines) where a central computer cannot feasibly reach all I/O. 5. **Open Standards:** Distributed systems embrace open protocols (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, OPC-UA), enabling **interoperability** between vendors — a cornerstone of **Industry 4.0**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q. (a)** Explain the functions of **Level 0, Level 1, and Level 2** of the Industrial Automation Architecture with suitable examples of components used at each level. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the major **communication protocols** used at different levels of the automation hierarchy? Explain **HART, Foundation Fieldbus, and OPC-UA** with their typical applications. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Functions of Levels 0, 1, 2 (7 marks):** - **Level 0 – Physical Process (2.5 marks):** This is the actual plant. Its function is to **transduce** physical phenomena into standardized signals and to **manipulate** process variables. *Sensors* convert physical quantities to electrical signals (e.g., **RTD** for temperature, **strain gauge** for pressure, **orifice plate + DP transmitter** for flow). *Actuators* convert control signals into physical action (e.g., **pneumatic diaphragm control valve** modulating a fluid flow, **VFD** varying a motor's speed, **solenoid** opening/closing a line). Standard signals are **4–20 mA** (live-zero) and **3–15 psi** (pneumatic). The **live-zero** principle allows the controller to distinguish a true zero (4 mA) from a broken wire (0 mA). - **Level 1 – Basic Control (2.5 marks):** This level **closes the control loop** in real time. The primary devices are the **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** for discrete/fast logic and the **DCS (Distributed Control System) controller** for continuous processes. Control algorithms include **PID, cascade, ratio, feedforward, override, and split-range**. A PID output is given by the ISA-standard equation: $$u(t) = K_c\!\left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$ Scan times are typically **10 ms to 1 s**. The controller compares the measured process variable (PV) with the setpoint (SP), computes the error, and adjusts the output to the actuator. - **Level 2 – Supervisory Control (2 marks):** This is the **operator's interface** to the plant. The **HMI (Human–Machine Interface)** provides animated mimics, trends, and alarm summaries. The **SCADA** system enables remote monitoring and control over geographically distributed assets. The **Historian** archives all process data (typically at 1-second resolution) for post-event analysis and regulatory compliance. Operators at this level can issue setpoint changes, acknowledge alarms, and start/stop equipment. --- **(b) Communication Protocols at Different Levels (7 marks):** The protocols differ in **bandwidth, determinism, and physical layer** to suit the latency requirements of each level. - **Field Level (L0–L1):** - **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer):** A **hybrid** protocol that overlays a digital **1200 bps** signal on top of the conventional **4–20 mA** loop. Allows simultaneous analog measurement + digital communication (device diagnostics, remote calibration). Widely used in **oil & gas and chemical plants** as a transitional technology. - **Foundation Fieldbus (FF):** A fully **digital, two-wire fieldbus** that replaces 4–20 mA entirely. Supports **distributed control blocks** — PID, arithmetic, integrator — that can execute *inside* the field device, freeing the controller. Used in **continuous process industries** (refineries, petrochemicals). Variants: **H1 (31.25 kbps)** for field devices and **HSE (High Speed Ethernet, 100 Mbps)** for the control backbone. - **Higher Levels (L2–L4):** - **OPC-UA (Open Platform Communications – Unified Architecture):** The **modern, vendor-neutral standard** for industrial data exchange. Runs on **TCP/IP**, supports **information modelling**, **encryption**, and **platform independence** (works on Windows, Linux, embedded, cloud). It is the **backbone of Industry 4.0**, enabling seamless data flow from sensor to ERP. It supports both **client-server** and **publish-subscribe** models. - **Other notable protocols:** **Modbus TCP** (simple, register-based, very common), **PROFINET** (deterministic, used with Siemens), **EtherNet/IP** (used with Rockwell/Allen-Bradley), and **EtherCAT** (extremely high speed, used in motion control). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing *sensor* with *transmitter*** (loses 0.5–1 mark). A sensor *senses*; a transmitter *standardizes* the signal. > 2. **Forgetting the live-zero concept** in 4–20 mA (loses 1 mark). Always mention that **4 mA = live zero**, distinguishing it from a broken wire at 0 mA. > 3. **Drawing only 3 levels instead of 5** (loses 2–3 marks). The examiner expects the **full five-level ISA-95 model** in the diagram. > 4. **Conflating PLC and DCS** (loses 0.5 mark). PLC = discrete/logic; DCS = continuous/integrated. SCADA is **NOT a controller** — it is a *supervisory* system. > 5. **No mention of information flow direction** (loses 1 mark). Always state that data flows *up* and commands flow *down*. > 6. **Omitting the network/protocol layer** in the block diagram (loses 1 mark). Draw the network as a separate block between levels. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Architecture = Layers + Networks + Protocols + Functions** — never just hardware. - **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** is the **only acceptable framework** — memorize all 5 levels and at least one component per level. - **Level 0** = physical (sensors, actuators); **Level 1** = control (PLC, DCS); **Level 2** = supervisory (SCADA, HMI); **Level 3** = operations (MES); **Level 4** = enterprise (ERP). - **4–20 mA live-zero** is the most-tested signal standard. Equation: $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{V - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}}$. - **Information flow is bidirectional** — measurements UP, setpoints DOWN. State this in every answer. - **Response time decreases** as you go up the hierarchy: **ms at L1 → seconds at L2 → minutes/hours at L4**. - **Distributed > Centralized** because of fault tolerance, scalability, and open standards. - **HART** is a hybrid analog+digital protocol; **Fieldbus** is fully digital; **OPC-UA** is the Industry-4.0 / cloud-ready standard. - **PLC** → discrete / fast logic; **DCS** → continuous / integrated; **SCADA** → wide-area telemetry (not a controller!). - **CIM (Computer Integrated Manufacturing)** is the older name for the full L0–L4 integration concept; **CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems)** and **IIoT** are its modern Industry-4.0 extensions. - **Industry 4.0** does **not** replace the hierarchy — it **augments** it with a parallel cloud/IIoT layer; the *response-time pyramid* still holds. - **Cybersecurity** is a modern cross-cutting concern: ISA-95 levels are also mapped to **ISA-99/IEC 62443** security zones — a frequent KTU follow-up question.

topics.title: Measurement Systems Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT744 - PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement Systems Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Measurement System

A **Measurement System** is an organized assembly of physical components, sensors, transducers, signal conditioners, and display units that converts a physical variable (the *measurand*) into a meaningful, quantifiable output (the *measurement*) for monitoring, control, and decision-making in industrial processes.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT744 – Process Automation)**, a measurement system is the **front-end of every process control loop**. The performance of the entire automation cascade — from sensor → transmitter → controller → final control element → process — is fundamentally limited by the **characteristics** of this initial measurement stage. This concept is popularly captured by the **Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)** principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The characteristics of measurement systems are classified into two broad categories:
> 1. **Static Characteristics** — Behavior when the measurand is constant or slowly varying (steady-state analysis).
> 2. **Dynamic Characteristics** — Behavior when the measurand is rapidly varying with time (transient analysis).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Stethoscope of a Plant

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The **stethoscope** is the doctor's *measurement system*. It converts the internal vibration of the heart (physical variable) into an audible sound (output signal). Now, several factors determine whether this diagnosis is reliable:

- **Accuracy**: Does the doctor's diagnosis match the true disease?
- **Precision**: Will two doctors using identical stethoscopes reach the same conclusion?
- **Sensitivity**: Can the stethoscope detect a faint murmur, or only loud abnormalities?
- **Time Lag (Dynamic Response)**: Does the stethoscope respond instantly, or does it have a delay?

In a chemical plant, the *stethoscope* is replaced by sensors and transmitters measuring **temperature, pressure, flow, and level**. The same principles of accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and dynamic response govern whether the automation system can react in time to maintain safe and efficient operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Measurand vs. Measured Value**
> - **Measurand**: The *true physical quantity* subjected to measurement (e.g., 100 °C actual temperature).
> - **Measured Value**: The *output indicated* by the instrument (e.g., 99.6 °C displayed value).
> - The difference is the **Error of Measurement**.

## 1.3 The Two Domains of Measurement System Characteristics

| Domain | Defining Condition | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static Characteristics** | Measurand is constant or varies extremely slowly | Calibration, steady-state tank level reading, pressure gauge |
| **Dynamic Characteristics** | Measurand varies rapidly with time | Flow fluctuations, transient temperature during reactor startup, step changes in setpoint |

**Static characteristics** answer: *"How close is my reading to the truth, and how consistently does it repeat?"*
**Dynamic characteristics** answer: *"How quickly and faithfully does my instrument follow a changing input?"*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

The following sub-sections analyze each static characteristic in the operational order required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each characteristic is defined, justified, and quantified.

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
- **Definition**: The degree of *closeness* of a measured value to the *true value* of the measurand.
- **Mathematical Form**: Expressed as **Inaccuracy** = Measured Value − True Value.
- **Why It Matters**: Determines the *systematic bias* of the instrument. Poor accuracy in a feedback loop causes persistent offset from the desired setpoint.
- **Why and How**: Accuracy is influenced by calibration errors, zero drift, and sensor aging. It is corrected by periodic re-calibration against a primary standard.

### 2.1.2 Precision
- **Definition**: The degree of *reproducibility* or *repeatability* of measurements under unchanging conditions.
- **Why It Matters**: A high-precision instrument gives nearly identical readings for the same input repeatedly. It is *not* the same as accuracy — a precise instrument can be consistently *wrong*.
- **Engineering Analogy**: A dart player who hits the same spot on the wall consistently is *precise*, but only accurate if that spot is the bullseye.

### 2.1.3 Resolution (Discrimination)
- **Definition**: The smallest *incremental change* in the measurand that produces a detectable change in the output.
- **Why It Matters**: Sets the *fineness* of measurement. A digital thermometer with **0.1 °C** resolution is superior to one with **1 °C** resolution.

### 2.1.4 Sensitivity
- **Definition**: The *ratio* of the change in output to the corresponding change in input.
- **Why It Matters**: Determines the *amplification* of the measurement signal. High sensitivity is desirable for small-signal measurands but must be balanced against noise pickup.

### 2.1.5 Range and Span
- **Range**: The minimum to maximum value of the measurand for which the instrument is designed.
- **Span**: The *algebraic difference* between the maximum and minimum of the range.

### 2.1.6 Linearity
- **Definition**: The degree to which the output of an instrument follows a *straight-line* relationship with the input.
- **Why It Matters**: Linear instruments are easier to calibrate, easier to interpret, and easier to integrate with controllers (PID tuning relies on linear models).

### 2.1.7 Hysteresis
- **Definition**: The phenomenon where the output differs for the *same input* depending on whether the input is *increasing* or *decreasing*.
- **Why It Matters**: Creates a *dead band* in control loops — the controller will not react to small input changes within the hysteresis loop, causing oscillations.

### 2.1.8 Dead Zone / Dead Band
- **Definition**: The range of input values for which *no change* is observed in the output.
- **Why It Matters**: Represents a *blind region* of the instrument — the process may be changing but the instrument shows nothing.

### 2.1.9 Repeatability and Reproducibility
- **Repeatability**: Variation in output when the *same input* is measured repeatedly under *identical conditions* by the *same observer*, using the *same instrument*.
- **Reproducibility**: Variation in output when the same input is measured under *changed conditions* — different observer, different location, different time.

### 2.1.10 Drift
- **Definition**: Slow, undesirable change in output with time, even when the input is held constant.
- **Types**: Zero drift (shift of the calibration intercept) and Span drift (change in slope).

### 2.1.11 Stability
- **Definition**: The ability of an instrument to *retain its performance characteristics* over a long period without re-calibration.

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Structured Analysis

When the measurand changes rapidly, the instrument's internal energy-storage elements (thermal mass, fluid inertia, electrical capacitance) cause the output to *lag* behind the input. The dynamic behavior is therefore modeled using **differential equations**.

### 2.2.1 Zero-Order System
- **Governing Equation**: $y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$
- **Physical Meaning**: *No energy storage* — output responds instantaneously to input. Example: a potentiometer measuring linear displacement.

### 2.2.2 First-Order System
- **Governing Differential Equation**: 
$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$
- **Physical Examples**: A mercury-in-glass thermometer (thermal lag), a single RC circuit.
- **Key Parameter**: **Time constant $\tau$** — the time required for the output to reach **63.2 %** of its final steady-state value in response to a step input.

### 2.2.3 Second-Order System
- **Governing Differential Equation**:
$$
\frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2}y(t) = K\omega_{n}^{2}x(t)
$$
- **Physical Examples**: A spring–mass–damper pressure sensor, a U-tube manometer, a control valve with actuator dynamics.
- **Key Parameters**: **Damping ratio $\zeta$** and **Natural frequency $\omega_{n}$**.

### 2.2.4 Transfer Function Representation
Taking the Laplace transform of the first-order differential equation (with zero initial conditions):

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

For the second-order system:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for Module 1 measurement characteristics. Use this as your **last-day revision sheet** before the KTU ESE.

| # | Characteristic | Formula / Definition | Typical Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Error} = \text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}$ | Expressed in $\% \text{ of full scale}$ |
| 2 | **Sensitivity** | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output units per input unit |
| 3 | **Span** | $\text{Span} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Same as input units |
| 4 | **Resolution** | Smallest $\Delta x$ producing detectable $\Delta y$ | Engineering judgement |
| 5 | **Static Error** | $e_{s} = y_{\text{measured}} - y_{\text{true}}$ | Vector difference |
| 6 | **Hysteresis** | $H = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | $\% \text{ of full scale}$ |
| 7 | **First-Order Transfer Function** | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\tau =$ time constant |
| 8 | **First-Order Step Response** | $y(t) = K\,A\,(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $A =$ step magnitude |
| 9 | **63.2 % Rule** | $y(\tau) = 0.632\,K\,A$ | Used to find $\tau$ experimentally |
| 10 | **Second-Order Transfer Function** | $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ | $\omega_{n}, \zeta$ are key |
| 11 | **Damped Natural Frequency** | $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ | Only for $0 \le \zeta < 1$ |
| 12 | **Peak Overshoot** | $M_{p} = e^{\left( -\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} \right)}$ | Fraction of final value |
| 13 | **Peak Time** | $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$ | Time to first peak |
| 14 | **Settling Time (2 % criterion)** | $t_{s} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | Approximate |
| 15 | **Rise Time (0 % to 100 %)** | $t_{r} \approx \dfrac{\pi - \theta}{\omega_{d}}$, with $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right)$ | First crossing |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Accuracy and Precision** govern the *quality* of data historians in pharmaceutical batch reporting (FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance).
- **Dynamic Characteristics** determine whether a flow sensor can capture **rapid transients** during emergency shutdowns (ESD) in oil & gas plants.
- **Hysteresis** is critical in **valve positioners** — excessive hysteresis leads to limit-cycling oscillations in feedback control.
- **Time-constant** values dictate **tuning parameters** for cascade and feedforward control loops.
- **Resolution** is paramount in **analytical instruments** like chromatographs detecting trace pollutants in water treatment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order System Step Response

### Given
The governing first-order differential equation for a measurement element:

$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$

where $\tau$ is the time constant and $K$ is the static sensitivity. The input is a *step* of magnitude $A$ applied at $t = 0$.

### Step 1: Apply the Step Input
A step input of magnitude $A$ is mathematically written as $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

For $t > 0$, the differential equation becomes:

$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot A
$$

### Step 2: Solve the Homogeneous (Transient) Part
The homogeneous equation is $\tau \dfrac{dy_{h}}{dt} + y_{h} = 0$.

Rearranging and integrating:

$$
\frac{dy_{h}}{y_{h}} = -\frac{1}{\tau}\,dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_{h}(t) = C_{1}\,e^{-t/\tau}
$$

### Step 3: Find the Particular (Steady-State) Solution
At steady state, $\dfrac{dy_{p}}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$
y_{p} = K \cdot A
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Initial Condition
Assume the instrument initially shows zero output, i.e., $y(0) = 0$. The general solution is:

$$
y(t) = y_{p} + y_{h} = K A + C_{1}\,e^{-t/\tau}
$$

Substituting $y(0) = 0$:

$$
0 = K A + C_{1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{1} = -K A
$$

### Step 5: Final Step Response Equation

$$
y(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

### Step 6: The 63.2 % Verification
Setting $t = \tau$:

$$
y(\tau) = K A \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = K A \left(1 - 0.3679\right) = 0.6321\,K A
$$

Thus at one time constant, the output reaches **63.2 %** of its final value. This is the famous **KTU 2024 numerical favourite** for experimental determination of $\tau$.

### Step 7: Time Required to Reach 99.3 % of Final Value (4$\tau$ Rule)

$$
y(4\tau) = K A \left(1 - e^{-4}\right) = K A \left(1 - 0.0183\right) = 0.9817\,K A
$$

After approximately $4\tau$ seconds, the output has effectively reached its steady-state value — this is the **settling time criterion** for first-order systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second-Order System Step Response (Underdamped Case)

### Given
The general second-order system transfer function is:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}
$$

A unit step input $X(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ is applied.

### Step 1: Compute the Output in the s-Domain

$$
Y(s) = G(s)\cdot X(s) = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s\,(s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2})}
$$

### Step 2: Factor the Denominator
The roots of the characteristic equation $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$ are:

$$
s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2} - 1}
$$

For the **underdamped case** ($0 \le \zeta < 1$), let us define the *damped natural frequency*:

$$
\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}
$$

So the roots become *complex conjugates*:

$$
s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\omega_{d}
$$

### Step 3: Partial Fraction Decomposition

$$
Y(s) = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K\!\left(s + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\right)}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}
$$

### Step 4: Inverse Laplace Transform to Time Domain

$$
y(t) = K\left[1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\,\sin\!\left(\omega_{d}t + \phi\right)\right]
$$

where $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right)$.

### Step 5: Derive Peak Overshoot $M_{p}$
Peak overshoot occurs when $\sin(\omega_{d}t + \phi) = 1$, i.e., $\omega_{d}t + \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$. Solving for the first peak time:

$$
t_{p} = \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
M_{p} = e^{\left(-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\right)}
$$

This is the **standard KTU 2024 formula** for percentage overshoot:

$$
\%M_{p} = 100 \cdot e^{\left(-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\right)}
$$

### Step 6: Settling Time (2 % Band)
The exponential envelope decays as $e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}$. The 2 % criterion requires:

$$
e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t_{s}} = 0.02 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{s} = \frac{-\ln(0.02)}{\zeta\omega_{n}} \approx \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Sensitivity and Resolution

Let the calibration curve of an instrument be $y = f(x)$, where $y$ is the output and $x$ is the input.

**Sensitivity** at any operating point is the *slope* of the calibration curve:

$$
K(x) = \frac{dy}{dx}
$$

**Resolution** $\delta x$ corresponds to the *minimum detectable change in output* $\delta y$, which is typically one least-count of the display. Therefore:

$$
\delta x = \frac{\delta y}{K(x)} = \frac{\delta y}{dy/dx}
$$

This shows that **higher sensitivity leads to finer resolution** — a fundamental design principle in instrumentation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Static Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is a **favourite KTU question** format. Memorize the distinctions.

| Characteristic | Affected by Time? | Depends on Input Range? | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Accuracy | No | Indirectly | $\% \text{FS}$ |
| Precision | No | No | $\% \text{FS}$ |
| Sensitivity | No | Yes | Output/Input |
| Resolution | No | Yes | Input units |
| Hysteresis | Yes (history) | Yes | $\% \text{FS}$ |
| Drift | Yes (slowly) | Yes | Output units / time |
| Linearity | No | Yes | Dimensionless |
| Dead Zone | No | Yes | Input units |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generalized Measurement System

The figure below represents the canonical KTU-approved block diagram of a complete measurement system. Each block performs a well-defined transformation on the signal as it flows from the physical process to the human operator or controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PRIMARY SENSING ELEMENT] --> nodeB[VARIABLE CONVERSION ELEMENT]
    nodeB --> nodeC[VARIABLE MANIPULATION ELEMENT]
    nodeC --> nodeD[DATA TRANSMISSION ELEMENT]
    nodeD --> nodeE[DATA PRESENTATION ELEMENT]
    nodeA -. Measurand .-> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeF[HUMAN OPERATOR OR CONTROLLER]
    nodeF -. Feedback .-> nodeA

    style nodeA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#C0A0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Explanation of Blocks:**
- **Primary Sensing Element**: Direct contact with the measurand (e.g., thermocouple junction, orifice plate).
- **Variable Conversion Element**: Converts the sensed phenomenon into a more usable form (e.g., thermocouple voltage).
- **Variable Manipulation Element**: Amplifies, filters, linearizes, or cold-junction compensates the signal.
- **Data Transmission Element**: Carries the signal (current loop 4–20 mA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus).
- **Data Presentation Element**: Displays the value (indicator, DCS screen, HMI).
- **Human Operator or Controller**: Closes the loop via feedback to the process.

---

## 4.2 Static vs Dynamic Characteristics — Classification Tree

The following diagram provides a structural taxonomy of all measurement-system characteristics covered in Module 1, organized as per the KTU 2024 syllabus hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode[MEASUREMENT SYSTEM CHARACTERISTICS] --> statNode[STATIC CHARACTERISTICS]
    rootNode --> dynNode[DYNAMIC CHARACTERISTICS]

    statNode --> accNode[Accuracy]
    statNode --> precNode[Precision]
    statNode --> sensNode[Sensitivity]
    statNode --> resNode[Resolution]
    statNode --> rangeNode[Range and Span]
    statNode --> linNode[Linearity]
    statNode --> hystNode[Hysteresis]
    statNode --> dzNode[Dead Zone]
    statNode --> repNode[Repeatability]
    statNode --> reprodNode[Reproducibility]
    statNode --> driftNode[Drift]
    statNode --> stabNode[Stability]

    dynNode --> zoNode[Zero Order System]
    dynNode --> foNode[First Order System]
    dynNode --> soNode[Second Order System]

    foNode --> foTFNode[Transfer Function K divided by tau s plus 1]
    foNode --> foSRNode[Step Response K A times 1 minus exp of minus t over tau]
    foNode --> foTCNode[Time Constant tau - 63.2 percent rule]

    soNode --> soTFNode[Transfer Function K omega n squared over denominator]
    soNode --> soDampNode[Damping Ratio zeta sub cases]
    soNode --> soPerfNode[Performance Indices Mp tp ts]

    style rootNode fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style statNode fill:#B0E0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dynNode fill:#B0C0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style soDampNode fill:#E0B0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Dynamic Response Behaviour for Different Damping Ratios

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zetaAxis[DAMPING RATIO ZETA] --> case1[ZETA EQUAL TO ZERO]
    zetaAxis --> case2[ZERO LESS THAN ZETA LESS THAN ONE]
    zetaAxis --> case3[ZETA EQUAL TO ONE]
    zetaAxis --> case4[ZETA GREATER THAN ONE]

    case1 --> r1[PURE OSCILLATORY UNDAMPED]
    case2 --> r2[UNDERDAMPED OSCILLATORY DECAY]
    case3 --> r3[CRITICALLY DAMPED FASTEST NON OSCILLATORY]
    case4 --> r4[OVERDAMPED SLOW NON OSCILLATORY]

    style zetaAxis fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style case1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style case2 fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style case3 fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style case4 fill:#C0A0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: In process instrumentation, **critical damping** ($\zeta = 1$) is rarely used. Most flow, pressure, and level sensors are designed to operate in the **underdamped regime** ($0.4 \le \zeta \le 0.8$) to balance speed of response against overshoot. Temperature sensors (first-order) are intentionally slowed to filter out noise.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sensitivity and Resolution of a measurement system. How are they related? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sensitivity** is defined as the ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in the input that causes it:

$$
\text{Sensitivity } K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}
$$

**Resolution** is the smallest incremental change in the input (measurand) that produces a detectable change in the output of the instrument. It is the *finest* change the instrument can resolve.

**Relationship**: For an instrument with calibration curve $y = f(x)$, resolution is given by:

$$
\delta x = \frac{\delta y}{dy/dx} = \frac{\delta y}{K}
$$

where $\delta y$ is the least count of the display. Hence, **higher sensitivity yields finer resolution**. *(1 mark for relationship derivation.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Repeatability and Reproducibility with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Repeatability | Reproducibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Closeness of agreement between successive readings for the same input under *identical* conditions. | Closeness of agreement between readings for the same input under *changed* conditions (observer, instrument, location, or time). |
| **Conditions** | Same observer, same instrument, same place, short time interval. | Different observer, or different instrument, or different environment, longer time interval. |
| **Example** | Reading a thermocouple 10 times within 1 hour in the same lab. | Reading the same thermocouple in the same lab after 6 months, after re-calibration. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of a generalized measurement system. List any **six static characteristics** of a measurement system and define any **four** of them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mercury-in-glass thermometer is a first-order measurement system with time constant $\tau = 12 \text{ s}$ and static sensitivity $K = 1 \text{ mm/°C}$. The thermometer is initially at $30 \text{ °C}$ and is suddenly immersed in a bath at $100 \text{ °C}$. Find:
   (i) The reading of the thermometer after $10 \text{ s}$.
   (ii) The time required to reach $95 \text{ °C}$.
   (iii) The maximum error if the reading is taken at $t = 30 \text{ s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a)** *Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).*

**Functional Elements of a Generalized Measurement System**:
1. **Primary Sensing Element** — senses the measurand.
2. **Variable Conversion Element** — converts measurand into a corresponding signal.
3. **Variable Manipulation Element** — amplifies, filters, linearizes.
4. **Data Transmission Element** — transmits signal to remote location.
5. **Data Presentation Element** — displays or records output.
*(1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for naming all elements.)*

**Six Static Characteristics**: Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Resolution, Linearity, Hysteresis, Dead Zone, Repeatability, Reproducibility, Drift, Stability. *(1 mark for list.)*

**Definitions (any four for 4 marks):**
- **Accuracy**: Closeness of measured value to true value.
